title: One-Board Magazine Rack
summary: A wall-mounted magazine rack built entirely from a single 1x3 board, angled so covers face outward.
description:
The whole rack — bottom, back, and two sides — comes from one 1x3 board, which makes this a good project for using up a single leftover length rather than buying anything new.

The back piece is deliberately **angled rather than vertical**, which is what tilts stored magazines forward so their covers face out and stay browsable, instead of standing straight up where only the spines show.

Pocket holes go in the bottom and back pieces only — the two side pieces stay clean on their visible faces, since they're what someone sees head-on when the rack is mounted.

cutList.note: Angled to tilt magazines forward, covers facing out.
Angled top edge to match the back piece.

steps.title: Cut the four pieces
steps.body: From the single 1x3, cut one bottom 10" long, one back 12" long, and two sides 9" long — a 1x3's actual size is 3/4" x 2 1/2", so all four pieces share that cross-section and only the lengths differ. That is 40" out of a 96" board, so you have plenty of material to recut a piece if an angle goes wrong. The back and the top edges of the two sides get an angled cut so the back leans instead of standing vertical, and that lean is what tilts magazines forward so their covers face out. Cut the two sides together, or set the saw once and cut them back to back, so their angles match exactly — a mismatch there twists the whole rack.

steps.title: Drill pocket holes
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes in the bottom piece and the back piece only, placing them on the faces that end up hidden inside the rack. The two sides stay clean — they are what you look straight at once the rack is mounted, and a pocket hole there is permanent. Two holes at each end of the bottom and of the back is enough on parts this narrow. Do all of this now, while both pieces are flat and loose; reaching into an assembled rack with a jig is far harder.

steps.title: Attach the bottom
steps.body: Stand the two 9" sides on edge and set the 10" bottom between them, flush with their lower ends, then drive the pocket screws outward from the bottom into the inside face of each side. Glue the joint first — 1x3 pine is narrow, and the screws are doing real work on something that will hang loaded on a wall. Watch that the sides stay square to the bottom as you drive the second screw; the first one can pull the assembly out of square. With the bottom in, the rack has its width and only needs the back to lock it up.

steps.title: Attach the angled back
steps.body: Dry-fit the back against the angled top edges of the sides before you drive anything — it is cut 12" long against a 10" bottom, so check how it actually lands on your assembly and trim it to fit rather than trusting the number. Once it fits, glue it and drive the pocket screws home so it leans back rather than standing upright. That lean is the whole point of the design, and the closed triangle it forms in section is also what stops the rack from racking. Wipe the squeeze-out now, because dried glue will show through paint.

steps.title: Fill, sand, and finish
steps.body: Press wood filler into the exposed pocket holes, let it dry fully, and sand it flush — filler shrinks a little as it dries, so a second thin pass is normal rather than a mistake. Sand every face and ease all the edges and corners, especially the front edges of the sides where hands reach past to pull a magazine out. Paint the rack inside as well as out; the inside faces show whenever it is empty.

steps.title: Mount
steps.body: Hold the rack against the wall where you want it, mark level, and fasten through the back piece into a stud wherever you can reach one — the back is the part with the best chance of landing on solid framing. If you are using adhesive strips instead, hang it low and keep the load light: a rack full of magazines is heavier than it looks, and strips let go all at once rather than gradually. Check it is level once more before the fasteners go fully home.
